# Tier 1 results — routing, mixture consistency, offset ensembling

**Generated by `report_tier1.py` from `cache_tier1/*.npz`. Do not hand-edit — rerun the script.**

Covers Tier 1 of the improvements roadmap. Counting is out of
scope here: every number below uses the **oracle speaker count** (`n_spks=N` is passed), so
this measures separation quality only and is unaffected by the counting question.

## What the metric is (and is not)

**SI-SNRi in dB — an IMPROVEMENT over the unprocessed mixture, not an absolute SDR.**
Computed with the authors' own `PIT_SISNRi(scale_inv=True)` from
`sr_corrnet/models/SR_CorrNet_SS/loss.py`, not a reimplementation.

**Do NOT compare these against the BSS_Eval SDR/SIR/SAR in `results_bleed.md`** — those are
absolute, these are improvements. That substitution is one step away from a wrong conclusion.

**Every comparison is PAIRED on identical mixture keys** (the intersection is computed, not
assumed). This matters more than sample size here: per-mixture difficulty carries a 1.5–4 dB
standard deviation, so an unpaired test at n=100 resolves ~±0.4 dB, while the paired delta
cancels that variance and resolves ~±0.03 dB. CIs are t-based 95%; p is a two-sided paired
t-test.

**Harness cross-check:** the `var-2-5spk` baseline column reproduces the verified baseline's headline
numbers exactly (23.39 / 23.84 / 22.20 / 20.57 at N=2/3/4/5, n=100), which is independent
evidence this harness agrees with the already-verified `benchmark.py`.

## 1.1 Checkpoint routing — VERDICT: route N=2 to `fix-2spk-l-dm`, nothing else

Dataset: **official WSJ0-mix `tt` (IN DOMAIN)**, oracle count. Baseline is
`shinuh/sr-corrnet-ss-1ch-wsj-var-2-5spk` (the currently shipped checkpoint).
A positive Δ means **the challenger beats the current checkpoint**.

| N | challenger checkpoint | var-2-5spk | challenger | paired Δ (dB) | 95% CI | p | n |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | `fix-2spk` | 23.39 | 22.24 | **-1.146** | ±0.777 | 0.004 | 100 |
| 2 | `fix-2spk-l-dm` | 23.39 | 23.96 | **+0.570** | ±0.150 | <0.001 | 100 |
| 2 | `var-2-3spk` | 23.39 | 22.49 | **-0.897** | ±0.768 | 0.022 | 100 |
| 3 | `var-2-3spk` | 23.84 | 21.93 | **-1.912** | ±0.741 | <0.001 | 100 |

### Verdict in words

**The roadmap's premise — "specialized models beat general ones at their own count" — is
FALSE here for three of the four checkpoints.** Only one routing rule survives:

- **`fix-2spk-l-dm` at N=2: +0.57 dB, p<0.001. SHIP THIS.** Stable across sample sizes
  (+0.529 at n=25 → +0.570 at n=100), so it is not a small-sample artifact.
- **`fix-2spk` at N=2: −1.15 dB. Do NOT route.** The plain specialized 2-speaker model is
  *worse* than the general one.
- **`var-2-3spk`: −0.90 at N=2, −1.91 at N=3. Do NOT route.**

**What this rules out, and what it implies.** The two `fix-2spk*` checkpoints differ only in
being large + dynamic-mixing. Plain specialization LOSES 1.15 dB; specialization + DM WINS
0.57 dB. So the win is attributable to **dynamic mixing, not to specialization** — which is
independent, measured support for Tier 2.1 (fine-tune with DM on), and evidence that the
remaining Tier 1 ceiling is low.

### The `var-2-3spk` N=3 failure is BIMODAL — a tail, not a slope

Worth recording because the mean hides it. Per-mixture at n=25, `var-2-3spk` minus
`var-2-5spk` at N=3:

- **13/25 mixtures are a tie** (within ±1 dB); **11/25 fail catastrophically** (−3 to −15 dB).
- **median Δ = −0.83 dB, mean Δ = −3.67 dB.** The mean is dragged entirely by the tail.
- `var-2-3spk`'s absolute scores collapse as low as **7.5 dB** on the failures.

This is a **reliability** failure, not a quality gap — so no sample size would rehabilitate
it. It is the strongest reason not to route N=3.

## 1.2 Mixture consistency — VERDICT: ship it, but it is worth ~+0.4 dB at N=2, ~0 at N=5

Δ = SI-SNRi(with MC) − SI-SNRi(without), **same forward pass** (so it costs no GPU and
carries no inference-time cost). Checkpoint `var-2-5spk`, oracle count.

| dataset | domain | N | baseline | +MC | paired Δ (dB) | 95% CI | p | n |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| WSJ0-mix `tt` | **in** | 2 | 23.39 | 23.81 | **+0.422** | ±0.028 | <0.001 | 100 |
| WSJ0-mix `tt` | **in** | 3 | 23.84 | 24.03 | **+0.187** | ±0.015 | <0.001 | 100 |
| WSJ0-mix `tt` | **in** | 4 | 22.20 | 22.28 | **+0.080** | ±0.010 | <0.001 | 100 |
| WSJ0-mix `tt` | **in** | 5 | 20.57 | 20.60 | **+0.038** | ±0.008 | <0.001 | 100 |
| LibriSpeech dev-clean | **out** | 2 | 18.47 | 18.85 | **+0.383** | ±0.086 | <0.001 | 100 |
| LibriSpeech dev-clean | **out** | 3 | 19.02 | 19.13 | **+0.102** | ±0.048 | <0.001 | 100 |
| LibriSpeech dev-clean | **out** | 4 | 18.30 | 18.29 | **-0.003** | ±0.045 | 0.898 | 100 |
| LibriSpeech dev-clean | **out** | 5 | 16.75 | 16.76 | **+0.012** | ±0.022 | 0.290 | 100 |

### The implementation trap — READ THIS BEFORE TOUCHING `mixture_consistency()`

**The textbook formula `s += (y - sum(s))/N` is WRONG for this model as written.**
Measured (`probe_output_scale.py`), not assumed:

- This model is trained with a **scale-invariant loss, so its output gain is arbitrary**.
  Raw streams are ~87× the true sources and **polarity-inverted** (best-fit gain ≈ **−0.011**,
  note the minus sign).
- Therefore `sum(est)` does **not** approximate the mixture in any domain — measured
  `||y_norm − sum(est)|| / ||y_norm||` = **9.6**, i.e. the naive residual is ~87× too large
  and annihilates the estimates.
- **SI-SNR would not catch this.** Being scale invariant, a wrong-domain `y` reads as an
  ordinary regression, not as a bug. This is the same class of silent failure as the
  clipping bug.

**The fix that makes it work:** the arbitrary gain is *global* — identical across streams to
within 1% (measured spread max/min = 1.00–1.01 at N=2/3/4). So one least-squares scalar
against the mixture restores consistency, and `mix` is available at test time (using the
references would be cheating). The blind estimate matches the reference-derived gain to 4
decimals (−0.011469 vs −0.01147).

**Bonus finding — `separate.py`'s shared-gain rescale is now VERIFIED, not assumed.** Because
the gain really is global, one shared gain across streams genuinely preserves relative speaker
levels. This was previously asserted; it is now measured.

### Verdict in words

Real, free, and always non-negative in domain — but **smaller than the +0.7 dB the paper
reports**, and it decays sharply with N (+0.42 → +0.04 from N=2 → N=5). Expected: the
residual is only ~3% for a model this strong, so there is little left to redistribute.

**Out of domain it is worth ~nothing above N=3** (+0.38 at N=2, but −0.003 at N=4 and +0.012
at N=5 — the N=4 point is a *negative* point estimate). Do not assume the in-domain gain
transfers.

## 1.3 Offset ensembling — VERDICT: real and grows with N, but costs 4×. NOT shipped

4 input offsets (0/16/32/48 samples), streams permutation- and sign-matched across runs,
then averaged. WSJ0 `tt`, `var-2-5spk`, oracle count. **Costs 4× inference.**
`ens+MC` applies mixture consistency on top of the ensemble, to test whether they stack.

| N | single | ensemble | paired Δ (dB) | 95% CI | p | ens+MC | Δ ens+MC | n | resolved? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 23.44 | 23.48 | **+0.042** | ±0.244 | 0.736 | 23.85 | **+0.411** ±0.254 | 100 | **NO — CI spans 0** |
| 3 | 23.93 | 24.18 | **+0.247** | ±0.081 | <0.001 | 24.33 | **+0.396** ±0.084 | 100 | yes |
| 4 | 22.28 | 22.71 | **+0.431** | ±0.246 | <0.001 | 22.74 | **+0.466** ±0.242 | 100 | yes |
| 5 | 20.60 | 21.08 | **+0.486** | ±0.264 | <0.001 | 21.07 | **+0.476** ±0.249 | 100 | yes |

### Is it worth 4× inference? Only at N≥3 — and only if the RTF budget allows

Counts whose 95% CI excludes zero: **[3, 4, 5]**. Counts still unresolved: **[2]**.

The decision is not "does ensembling help" but **"does it help on top of mixture
consistency, which is free?"** MC alone is the thing to beat:

| N | ens only | MC only (free) | ens+MC | what 4× inference actually buys over MC |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | +0.042 (ns) | +0.422 | +0.411 | **nothing — MC alone is better** | 
| 3 | +0.247 | +0.187 | +0.396 | **+0.21** | 
| 4 | +0.431 | +0.080 | +0.466 | **+0.39** | 
| 5 | +0.486 | +0.038 | +0.476 | **+0.44** | 

**They do stack at N=3/4/5** (roughly additively), but the two mechanisms trade places as N
rises: MC does all the work at N=2 and almost none at N=5, while ensembling does the
reverse. **At N=2 ensembling is strictly pointless** — `ens+MC` (+0.411) is no better than
MC alone (+0.422) and costs 4× inference for it.

### The gain grows with N — now confirmed at n=100, and mechanistically expected

Ensemble-only delta at n=100: **+0.042 → +0.247 → +0.431 → +0.486** at N=2/3/4/5 — the
mirror image of MC (**+0.42 → +0.19 → +0.08 → +0.04**), which shrinks with N.

That is consistent with `results_bleed.md`'s finding that the error is **SAR-type
(artifacts)**: artifacts are partly stochastic across offsets, so they cancel under
averaging while the signal reinforces. High-N mixtures have the most artifacts and the most
to gain. **The growth is real but it plateaus at the top end** (+0.431 → +0.486 from N=4 to
N=5, not the continued climb the n=25 pass suggested).

### ⚠ n=25 overestimated the ensemble at EVERY count — the cautionary tale of this document

| N | n=25 said | n=100 says | error |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | +0.204 | +0.042 | **-0.162** |
| 3 | +0.345 | +0.247 | **-0.098** |
| 4 | +0.587 | +0.431 | **-0.156** |
| 5 | +0.767 | +0.486 | **-0.281** |

**The bias is systematic and one-directional — every count overestimated, by 0.10–0.28 dB.**
At N=2 the n=25 figure was **+0.204 with a deceptively tight ±0.030 CI**, which looked like
a clean win; at n=100 it is **+0.042 ± 0.241**, spanning zero.

**Root cause: `sorted(keys)[:limit]` is not a random sample.** WSJ0-mix keys lead with a
speaker id, so the alphabetically-first mixtures all share a leading speaker (`050`), and
the first 25 are nearly a single session (`050a05*`). That subset is far more homogeneous
than the corpus. Two lessons:

1. **A tight CI at small n is not evidence of a real effect** — it can mean the sample was
   unrepresentative. The pairing trick that made n=25 sufficient elsewhere is weakest here,
   because two *different configurations* disagree per-mixture far more than one
   configuration disagrees with itself, giving the ensemble deltas heavy tails n=25 hid.
2. **Use `--seed` for anything new.** `tier1_eval.py --seed N` draws a random sample
   (the same seed gives the same mixtures across checkpoints, preserving pairing).

**This did NOT invalidate the shipped wins — see "Sampling" below, where a random sample
makes both of them slightly LARGER.** The distortion is specific to ensembling.

### The trap in the implementation

Two silent failure modes are handled in `tier1_ensemble.py`; both would quietly destroy the
result rather than error:

1. **Sign.** Each pass has its own arbitrary gain *including polarity*. Averaging runs
   without sign matching lets opposite polarities **cancel the signal**. Every run is
   signed-least-squares projected onto run 0.
2. **Permutation.** Stream order is not stable across independent runs — speaker 1 in run 0
   can emerge as speaker 3 in run 1. Streams are Hungarian-matched on |cosine| first.
   (Note this is *across runs*; there is no permutation drift across *time* — see 1.4.)

## 1.4 Chunk stitching / permutation alignment — VERDICT: NOT A BUG, no work needed

**Resolved by reading the source, no GPU required.** The roadmap flagged this as a possible
live bug ("speaker 1 in chunk A can emerge as speaker 3 in chunk B"). It cannot happen:

- `inference.py:process_waveform` → `engine_infer._single_pass_session` → `infer_chunk`
  forwards the **entire STFT in one pass**. There is no chunking, therefore no stitching,
  therefore no cross-boundary permutation drift.
- The docstring agrees: *"This release does not support CSS chunking; for long-form audio,
  process in segments."*

**The risk this leaves is different from the one the roadmap described.** Long audio is not
*scrambled*; it is passed whole to a model trained on 4 s (`max_len: 32000` @ 8 kHz), which is
untested. If the judge uses long audio, the open question is **quality degradation on long
input**, and any segmenting *you* add would then introduce the permutation problem yourself.
Still untested — an open risk.

## 1.7 Input preprocessing — VERDICT: correct, no work needed

Verified rather than assumed:

- **Resampling anti-aliases properly.** `librosa.resample` defaults to `res_type='soxr_hq'`
  (librosa 0.11.0). Probed with tones above the 4 kHz Nyquist: a 6 kHz tone resampled 16k→8k
  comes back at **rms 0.0015**, and 7 kHz at **0.0007**, versus ~0.707 if they were aliasing —
  ~54 dB of suppression. No naive decimation.
- **Shape** is `(1, N)` for mono, as required.
- **Normalization** is applied exactly once, internally, by `process_waveform`
  (`waveform / (waveform.std(dim=-1, keepdim=True) + 1e-8)`). Callers must not pre-normalize.

## What shipped, and how to use it

`pipeline.py` is the shipping path (`Separator`), used by both `separate.py` and
`benchmark.py`. Routing + MC are **ON by default in `separate.py`** and **OFF by default in
`benchmark.py`** — deliberately: the bare `benchmark.py` command
must keep reproducing the 23.39/23.84/22.20/20.57 baseline rather than silently change
meaning.

```bash
python separate.py input.wav -o out/              # routing + MC on; writes manifest.json
python separate.py input.wav -o out/ --no-route --no-mc   # plain baseline
python benchmark.py --root data/wsj0_kmix --limit 100          # baseline (unchanged)
python benchmark.py --root data/wsj0_kmix --limit 100 --route --mc   # with the wins
```

**Verified end-to-end, not just in the cache:** on the same 20 mixtures at N=2,
`benchmark.py` gives **22.99 dB** default and **23.99 dB** with `--route --mc`, matching the
caches exactly. `separate.py` on a 2-speaker mixture auto-routed to `fix-2spk-l-dm` with no
hint, and the written audio has **crest factor 21.98/18.76 dB** (natural speech; ~2 dB would
mean clipped) with **per-stream peaks that differ** (0.90 vs 0.80), confirming relative
speaker levels survive the shared-gain rescale.

**Routing infers N safely.** Routing needs the count *before* separating, and the count comes
from the general model. That is sound because the built-in counter **never under-predicts**
(0.0% below the diagonal over 2000 mixtures): predicted 2 ⇒ true ≤ 2 ⇒ true = 2.
So a predicted 2 is trustworthy **even out of domain**, where raw N=2 counting accuracy is
only 73%. Cost: one extra forward pass at RTF ~0.05 for 2-speaker input.

## Reproduce / resume

All runs are **resumable, and the reproduce command IS the resume command** — no `--resume`
flag exists. Rerunning computes only what is missing; a fully-cached rerun costs a second
because the check happens before the model loads. Raising `--limit` recomputes that count.

```bash
PY=SR_CorrNet_SS/.venv/Scripts/python.exe

# 1.1 routing + 1.2 mixture consistency (both from one forward pass)
$PY tier1_eval.py --repo shinuh/sr-corrnet-ss-1ch-wsj-var-2-5spk \
    --root data/wsj0_kmix --n-spks 2 3 4 5 --limit 100 --out cache_tier1/wsj0_var25.npz
$PY tier1_eval.py --repo shinuh/sr-corrnet-ss-1ch-wsj-fix-2spk-l-dm \
    --root data/wsj0_kmix --n-spks 2 --limit 100 --out cache_tier1/wsj0_fix2ldm.npz
$PY tier1_eval.py --repo shinuh/sr-corrnet-ss-1ch-wsj-fix-2spk \
    --root data/wsj0_kmix --n-spks 2 --limit 100 --out cache_tier1/wsj0_fix2.npz
$PY tier1_eval.py --repo shinuh/sr-corrnet-ss-1ch-wsj-var-2-3spk \
    --root data/wsj0_kmix --n-spks 2 3 --limit 100 --out cache_tier1/wsj0_var23.npz
$PY tier1_eval.py --repo shinuh/sr-corrnet-ss-1ch-wsj-var-2-5spk \
    --root data/libri_kmix_test --n-spks 2 3 4 5 --limit 100 --out cache_tier1/libri_var25.npz

# 1.3 offset ensembling (+ the ens+MC stacking column). ~15 min GPU, 4x inference.
$PY tier1_ensemble.py --root data/wsj0_kmix --n-spks 2 3 4 5 --limit 100 \
    --out cache_tier1/ensemble.npz

# Sampling robustness check: --seed draws a RANDOM sample instead of the
# alphabetically-first (which shares a leading speaker). Same seed => same
# mixtures across checkpoints, so the comparison stays paired.
$PY tier1_eval.py --repo shinuh/sr-corrnet-ss-1ch-wsj-var-2-5spk \
    --root data/wsj0_kmix --n-spks 2 3 4 5 --limit 100 --seed 1234 \
    --out cache_tier1/rand_var25.npz
$PY tier1_eval.py --repo shinuh/sr-corrnet-ss-1ch-wsj-fix-2spk-l-dm \
    --root data/wsj0_kmix --n-spks 2 --limit 100 --seed 1234 \
    --out cache_tier1/rand_fix2ldm.npz

# regenerate this document from the caches (CPU, seconds)
$PY report_tier1.py
```

**Note `PYTHONIOENCODING=utf-8`** if printing these tables to a Windows console; the file
itself is written with `encoding='utf-8'` and is immune.

## Where the raw data lives

| path | what |
|---|---|
| `cache_tier1/wsj0_*.npz` | per-mixture SI-SNRi (`base_N`, `mc_N`), keys, RTF — WSJ0 |
| `cache_tier1/libri_var25.npz` | same, LibriSpeech dev-clean (out of domain) |
| `cache_tier1/rand_*.npz` | the `--seed 1234` random-sample robustness check |
| `cache_tier1/ensemble.npz` | per-mixture `single_N`/`ens_N`/`ensmc_N` — **n=100 at N=2/3/4, n=25 at N=5** |
| `pipeline.py` | **the shipping path**: routing table + mixture consistency + safe rescale |
| `probe_output_scale.py` | evidence for the output-gain finding in 1.2 (~30 s GPU) |
| `tier1_eval.py` | routing + MC harness (resumable, atomic) |
| `tier1_ensemble.py` | offset ensembling (resumable, atomic) |
| `report_tier1.py` | regenerates this file from the caches |

Per-mixture arrays are keyed by filename, so any pair of caches can be re-compared paired
without touching the GPU.

## Sampling — the default subset is NOT random, and it was checked

Every table above except this section uses `sorted(keys)[:limit]`, matching `benchmark.py`
and the verified baseline (so the numbers stay comparable). **That
is not a random sample:** WSJ0-mix keys lead with a speaker id, so all 100 mixtures share a
leading speaker (`050`). The `tt` set has only **16 distinct speakers total**, and the first
100 keys do cover all 16 — so the subset is less degenerate than it first looks, but it is
still a biased 100 of 3000.

**The two shipped wins were re-measured on a random sample and both got LARGER**
(`--seed 1234`, n=100, paired):

| measurement | sorted prefix | random sample |
|---|---|---|
| routing, `l-dm` vs `var-2-5spk` @ N=2 | +0.570 ±0.150 | **+0.615 ±0.101** (p=<0.001) |
| mixture consistency @ N=2 | +0.422 ±0.028 | **+0.579 ±0.040** |
| mixture consistency @ N=3 | +0.187 ±0.015 | **+0.210 ±0.013** |
| mixture consistency @ N=4 | +0.080 ±0.010 | **+0.087 ±0.012** |
| mixture consistency @ N=5 | +0.038 ±0.008 | **+0.044 ±0.009** |

**Verdict: the shipped wins are robust to sampling** — the sorted subset was if anything
mildly *conservative* about them. The small-sample distortion documented in 1.3 is specific
to ensembling's heavy-tailed deltas, not a flaw in the routing or MC results.

Note the *absolute* baseline differs between subsets (N=2 base 23.39 sorted vs 23.98 random),
i.e. the sorted subset is slightly harder. **Absolute numbers are subset-dependent; the
paired deltas are what transfer.**

## Precision — what these numbers do and do not support

At n=100 paired, the CIs are **±0.01–0.15 dB** for MC and **±0.15–0.78 dB** for routing
(routing CIs are wider because two different checkpoints disagree per-mixture far more than
one checkpoint disagrees with itself).

- **Ample** for the +0.57 dB routing win and every MC row.
- **Do not read the third decimal.**
- **Every table is now n=100.** The ensemble's n=25 pass overestimated at every count (1.3);
  those figures are superseded and must not be quoted.
- All WSJ0 numbers are **in domain**. The `-l-dm` routing win is measured on WSJ0 only and
  **was not tested out of domain** — if the judge's audio is not WSJ0-like, it is unverified.

## Bottom line

**Confirmed, shipped, and verified end-to-end: +1.00 dB at N=2**, +0.19 at N=3, +0.08 at N=4,
+0.04 at N=5 — **at no extra inference cost.** Measured through the shipping path on identical
mixtures: `benchmark.py --n-spks 2 --limit 20` gives **22.99 dB** by default and **23.99 dB**
with `--route --mc`, both reproducing the caches exactly.

**Offset ensembling is real but marginal, and deliberately NOT shipped.** Measured at n=100
across all counts, *over mixture consistency alone*, it is worth **nothing at N=2, +0.21 at
N=3, +0.39 at N=4, +0.44 at N=5** — for **4× inference**. The gain grows with N (the mirror
image of MC), consistent with an SAR/artifact error that partly cancels under averaging. It
is left out because 4× inference for ≤0.44 dB is an RTF-budget tradeoff only the judge's
constraints can settle; `tier1_ensemble.py` implements it if that changes.

This is well short of the roadmap's "+1 to +3 dB" estimate for Tier 1, and the shape of the
result says why: **three of four routing bets lost; the one that won looks like a dynamic
mixing effect rather than a specialization effect; ensembling survives only at N≥3 and only
at 4× inference; and 1.4 and 1.7 — two of the seven items — turned out to be non-problems**
that cost nothing but were worth nothing. Tier 1 is close to exhausted. The remaining
leverage is **Tier 0** (what does
the judge actually test on — worth up to 9 dB, and gates everything) and **Tier 2.1**
(fine-tune with DM on), which the `-l-dm` result now independently supports: the only
checkpoint that beat the general model is the dynamic-mixing one.
